mechanism_narrative: Industrial production (INDPRO) is the key real-time indicator of manufacturing activity; when it declines, it signals economic contraction and lower corporate earnings, leading to negative S&P 500 forward returns over the next month. This signal is always active and requires no threshold.
failure_condition: If INDPRO declines over a 3-month period but the S&P 500 has positive 20-day returns more than 50% of the time in those periods, the hypothesis is falsified.

mechanism_narrative: When the unemployment rate rises above a critical threshold of 7.5%, it indicates severe labor market deterioration that further weakens consumer spending and corporate profits, causing negative S&P 500 forward returns over the next 20 trading days. This signal is only active when the threshold is crossed.
failure_condition: If UNRATE exceeds 7.5% and the S&P 500 produces positive 20-day returns in more than 60% of subsequent instances, the hypothesis is rejected.

mechanism_narrative: The interaction between declining industrial production (INDPRO) and falling housing starts (HOUST) jointly signals a deep, synchronized contraction in both manufacturing and residential investment. This confluence amplifies recession fears and leads to negative S&P 500 forward returns over a short horizon of 5 trading days.
failure_condition: If both INDPRO and HOUST decline month-over-month but the S&P 500 forward 5-day return is positive more than 50% of the time, the hypothesis fails.

mechanism_narrative: When the economy is in a late-recession regime with near-zero policy rates, a stabilization or increase in nonfarm payrolls (PAYEMS) signals that the labor market is bottoming. This improvement in employment conditions anticipates a recovery in corporate earnings and produces positive S&P 500 returns over the following quarter.
regime_hint: late recession with near-zero policy rates
failure_condition: If PAYEMS rises month-over-month during the late-recession regime but the S&P 500 forward 60-day return is negative more than 50% of the time, the hypothesis is invalid.

mechanism_narrative: A very low federal funds rate (near zero) reduces the discount rate applied to future equity cash flows, making stocks more attractive relative to bonds. This accommodative monetary policy stance signals central bank support, driving positive S&P 500 returns over a 60-day horizon regardless of other conditions.
failure_condition: If FEDFUNDS is below 0.5% but the S&P 500 forward 60-day return is negative more than 40% of the time, the hypothesis is rejected.

mechanism_narrative: When industrial production falls below the symbolic level of 100 (index), it indicates that manufacturing activity has contracted significantly below its 2017 base, signaling deepening recession and further downside for equities over the next week.
failure_condition: If INDPRO drops below 100 but the S&P 500 forward 5-day return is positive in more than 55% of cases, the hypothesis is falsified.

mechanism_narrative: The combination of near-zero policy rates (FEDFUNDS) and elevated unemployment (UNRATE) reveals a liquidity trap where monetary easing fails to stimulate demand. This interaction signals persistent economic weakness and predicts negative S&P 500 returns over a one-month horizon.
failure_condition: If both conditions hold but the S&P 500 forward 20-day return is positive more than 50% of the time, the hypothesis is invalid.

mechanism_narrative: In a regime of potential deflation, a month-over-month decline in the CPI signals falling aggregate demand and risk of a deflationary spiral. This discourages consumption and investment, leading to negative S&P 500 forward returns over a 60-day horizon. The signal is activated only when CPI declines month-over-month.
failure_condition: If CPIAUCSL decreases month-over-month but the S&P 500 forward 60-day return is positive in more than 50% of such instances, the hypothesis is rejected.
